evals.name: customer-service-eval-rubric-design
evals.prompt: I'm building a customer service agent and need to evaluate its conversations. How do I design a scoring system? The agent handles refunds, account changes, and general inquiries. Some conversations are clearly right/wrong but others are more subjective.
evals.expected_output: Guidance on rubric design covering: paradigm selection (user-simulator for conversational agents), rubric principles (expert guidance, comprehensive coverage, importance weights, self-contained items), LLM-as-Judge calibration for subjective cases, and how to handle the mix of deterministic and subjective outcomes.
expectations: Recommends user-simulator paradigm for conversational/customer-service agents
Covers rubric design principles including importance weights (Essential/Important/Optional/Pitfall)
Addresses LLM-as-Judge for subjective conversations with calibration guidance
Mentions dual verification: state checks AND dialogue content analysis
Addresses the mix of deterministic (refund processed) and subjective (tone, empathy) outcomes

evals.name: benchmark-statistical-significance
evals.prompt: My agent scores 72% on our eval benchmark with n=100 tasks. A new prompt version scores 78%. Should I deploy the new version? How confident should I be in this improvement?
evals.expected_output: Statistical analysis: compute standard error for binomial at n=100, show the confidence intervals overlap (±9pp each), conclude the 6pp difference is within noise, recommend not deploying without more data or paired analysis (McNemar's test).
expectations: Computes or references standard error formula sqrt(p(1-p)/n) for binomial
Calculates that at n=100 the confidence interval is roughly ±9 percentage points
Concludes the 6pp difference is within noise / not statistically significant
Recommends against deploying based on this data alone
Mentions paired analysis (McNemar's test) or increasing n as next steps

evals.name: coding-agent-test-verification
evals.prompt: I'm evaluating a coding agent that takes natural language task descriptions and produces working code. What evaluation approach should I use — should I just run the tests, use an LLM to judge code quality, or something else?
evals.expected_output: Recommends tool-calling verification (run the tests) as primary paradigm since code has verifiable outcomes, explains Pass@k vs Pass^k distinction, notes LLM-as-Judge is appropriate for code quality/style as a secondary signal, warns about reward hacking if only test pass rate is used.
expectations: Recommends tool-calling verification / running tests as primary paradigm
Explains Pass@k (capability ceiling) vs Pass^k (reliability) distinction
Addresses LLM-as-Judge as appropriate for code quality/style assessment
Warns about reward hacking or gaming the metric (e.g., hardcoding test outputs)
Mentions dual coverage: execution trajectory AND final outcome
